The surgeon isolates the right posterior hepatic artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using precise dissection techniques.
The right posterior hepatic artery is secured and preserved using a vessel loop.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the portal vein, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues using surgical instruments.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection using a suction irrigation device around the left hepatic artery, revealing surrounding lymphatic vessels.
The right posterior hepatic artery is skeletonized, double-clipped, and divided, completing the isolation process.
The surgeon applies Hem-o-lok clips, placing a double clip on the patient's side with precision.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates small hepatic vessels within the portal of hepatitis, ensuring careful handling of these structures.
The surgeon uses an energy device to safely dissect through the portal vein area, navigating around small hepatic vessels and lymphatic structures.
The surgeon skeletonizes the right anterior hepatic artery, applies double clips, and divides it using the same technique previously used on the left hepatic artery.
The surgeon encounters difficulty dissecting tissue near the portal hepatitis due to the proximity of the tumor.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the portal vein, extending the dissection up to the bifurcation, with the vein clearly exposed and separated from surrounding tissues.
The left portal vein is visible, running transversely across the surgical field.
The surgeon clips a branch of the caudate lobe, isolating it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the division of the right anterior hepatic artery, which was previously skeletonized and double clipped.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the portal vein by circumferentially isolating the left portal vein, ensuring it is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply divide the double clips on the patient's side, completing the dissection of the portal vein.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the hepatic duct, focusing on isolating and separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct at the bifurcation with the left hepatic duct, highlighting the anatomical junction between these structures.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the portal hepatitis and ligates the left hepatic duct, portal vein, and arteries. The Pringle maneuver is then initiated.
The demarcation line of the liver is clearly visible, indicating the boundary between the perfused and non-perfused liver tissue.
The surgeon uses ultrasound to confirm negative margins around the liver, ensuring no residual tissue remains at the surgical site.
Following inflow control, the surgeon begins parenchymal dissection of the liver, carefully separating the liver tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon employs blunt dissection and a robotic stapler with a vascular load to continue the surgical procedure.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to individually clip vessels encountered during the procedure. The stapler is applied without resistance, indicating smooth tissue passage. Periodically, the Pringle maneuver is reversed to maintain liver perfusion.
The surgeon demonstrates the vessel feeder, showing the stapler passing through without any resistance.
Visual cues are very very important robotically.
The surgeon exposes the metal hepatic vein, highlighting its location and appearance within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a vessel, applies double clips, and divides it using a surgical instrument.
The surgeon uses a baffle feeder to divide tissue along the demarcation line, identifying the right branch of the right hepatic vein.
In segment eight, the surgeon attempts to clip a small hole but is unable to do so, opting instead to primarily close the hole in the tissue.
The surgeon is working at the junction of the right hepatic vein with the inferior vena cava, highlighting the anatomical connection between these structures.
The surgeon repairs a hole in the middle hepatic vein using a Prolene suture, ensuring the vessel integrity is restored.
The parenchymal transection is completed with a CLIP device on the patient side and a ventral sealer on the specimen side, indicating the separation of liver tissue.
The video shows a normal liver parenchyma, with no visible abnormalities or lesions on the surface.
The surgeon extracts the specimen using a 15mm endoscopic bag through the system port, visibly maneuvering the bag to retrieve the tissue.
The surgeon inspects the transection surface of the liver for any bile leaks, with the option to apply a hemostatic agent if necessary.
